subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Percent of Eligible Voters by Generation, 2016–2036
stimulus: Source: Pew Research, 2020. Bar graph showing Percent of Eligible Voters by Generation from 2016 to 2036 (projected), including the Silent Generation, Baby Boomers, Generation X, Millennials, and Generation Z. *Note: Generation Z data also include those born after 2012.

parts.prompt: Identify the generation that is projected to have the lowest percentage of eligible voters in 2028, as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Silent Generation earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the generation that is projected to have the lowest percentage of eligible voters in 2028, as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies the Silent Generation as the generation projected to have the lowest percentage of eligible voters in 2028.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies the Silent Generation as the generation projected to have the lowest percentage of eligible voters in 2028.

parts.prompt: Describe the trend over time in the data, as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of a generational trend in the data. The response must describe a trend, not just a snapshot.

scoring_points.description: Describe the trend over time in the data, as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: Over time, the percentage of eligible voters increases among people born later.
alternatives.correct_example: Over time, the percentage of eligible voters increases among people born later.

required_elements: Over time, the percentage decreases among people born earlier.
alternatives.correct_example: Over time, the percentage decreases among people born earlier.

required_elements: Over time, the percentage of eligible voters among the Silent Generation decreases while the percentage of eligible voters among Generation Z increases.
alternatives.correct_example: Over time, the percentage of eligible voters among the Silent Generation decreases while the percentage of eligible voters among Generation Z increases.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about how a trend in the data could affect a 2032 presidential candidate's strategy to mobilize eligible voters.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must go beyond describing the trend to drawing a specific conclusion about campaign strategy implications. Accept any plausible and accurate strategic conclusion.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about how a trend in the data could affect a 2032 presidential candidate's strategy to mobilize eligible voters.

required_elements: As the percentage of eligible voters in Generation Z increases, a campaign could shift to a media platform Generation Z uses more, such as social media.
alternatives.correct_example: As the percentage of eligible voters in Generation Z increases, a campaign could shift to a media platform Generation Z uses more, such as social media.

required_elements: As the percentage of eligible voters among Baby Boomers decreases, a campaign could shift its platform to move away from issues that Baby Boomers care about the most.
alternatives.correct_example: As the percentage of eligible voters among Baby Boomers decreases, a campaign could shift its platform to move away from issues that Baby Boomers care about the most.

parts.prompt: Explain how life cycle effects, as shown in the bar graph, could influence a candidate's policy platform.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between generational composition of the electorate and policy platform choices — not just describe demographic changes.

scoring_points.description: Explain how life cycle effects, as shown in the bar graph, could influence a candidate's policy platform.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how life cycle effects influence policy platforms. Accept: as a generation gets older and its share of overall eligible voters changes, the issues that matter most to the public may change, and a candidate would respond by adopting popular positions on those issues; younger generations becoming a larger share of eligible voters could push candidates to address issues like climate change, student debt, or social issues that younger voters prioritize.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains how life cycle effects influence policy platforms. Accept: as a generation gets older and its share of overall eligible voters changes, the issues that matter most to the public may change, and a candidate would respond by adopting popular positions on those issues; younger generations becoming a larger share of eligible voters could push candidates to address issues like climate change, student debt, or social issues that younger voters prioritize.
